These materials help reduce waste and lower energy FULL CONTENT: [Register Today](http://registration.gwgci.org) ![Gould Construction Institute](http://www.gwgci.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/Gould-logo_f_horiz-tag-01.svg) ![](https://www.gwgci.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/ABC_logo_BLUE.svg) ![](https://www.gwgci.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/BUILDMASS_logo_BLUE.svg) # 2/3/25 – The Future of Green Building: Sustainable Practices in Construction ### **The Future of Green Building: Sustainable Practices in Construction** The construction industry is at a crossroads, where sustainability is no longer optional but essential. Green building practices are rapidly gaining momentum, driven by the need to reduce environmental impact, lower energy consumption, and create healthier spaces for occupants. In this post, we’ll explore the future of green building, including eco-friendly materials, energy-efficient design, and the evolving technologies shaping the industry. #### The Rise of Sustainable Practices Construction has traditionally been a resource-intensive industry with significant environmental impact. However, increasing awareness of climate change and resource depletion has pushed the industry toward more sustainable practices. Green building focuses on minimizing these impacts by using environmentally friendly materials and construction methods, reducing energy consumption, and lowering carbon footprints. #### Eco-Friendly Materials One of the cornerstones of green building is the use of sustainable materials. Today, builders are opting for materials that are renewable, recyclable, or locally sourced. Popular eco-friendly materials include bamboo, reclaimed wood, recycled steel, and low-VOC paints. These materials help reduce waste and lower energy consumption without compromising the quality or aesthetics of a building. #### Energy-Efficiency in Design Designing for energy efficiency is another key aspect of green building. Architects and engineers are increasingly incorporating energy-efficient strategies into their designs. This includes passive solar design, better insulation, energy-efficient windows, and green roofs. By reducing a building’s energy demand, these strategies help cut down on utility costs and reduce overall environmental impact. #### Green Certifications and Standards Certifications like LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) and WELL have become the benchmark for sustainable buildings. These certifications ensure that buildings meet strict environmental and health criteria. Green certifications not only help reduce a building’s environmental footprint but also increase its marketability, as more businesses and homeowners are prioritizing sustainability. #### The Future of Green Building The future of green building is promising, with innovations in technology and design pushing the boundaries of what’s possible. Smart homes, net-zero buildings, and regenerative designs are all on the horizon. Additionally, sustainable construction practices are being integrated into every phase of a project, from planning and design to demolition and recycling. As the world moves toward more sustainable practices, the construction industry must adapt. Green building practices are not just a trend; they are the future. By embracing eco-friendly materials, energy-efficient designs, and cutting-edge technologies, the construction industry can create a more sustainable, environmentally responsible future. First name Last name Email address Job title Company name Industry 16 sustainable and cost-effective building materials ==================================================== **Posted on:** 8 May, 2024 This article may refer to the University College of Estate Management (UCEM) which became University of the Built Environment in June 2025. ![Image 2](https://www.ube.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/16-sustainable-and-cost-effective-building-materials-article.jpg) _These 16 innovative and eco-friendly materials can help the construction industry address its role in the climate crisis._ * * * The built environment is one of the single most pollutive sectors in the world, but it’s the construction industry in particular that is causing the most damage to the environment. [Embodied carbon](https://www.ube.ac.uk/whats-happening/articles/what-is-embodied-carbon/) – the carbon dioxide emissions created in the construction process before a building becomes operational – accounts for [10% of all global energy-related CO2 emissions](https://journal-buildingscities.org/articles/10.5334/bc.257#:~:text=From%20the%20perspective%20of%20a,the%20embodied%20emissions%20of%20buildings.). What’s more, the production of materials like concrete is extremely energy-intensive, accounting for [8% of global CO2](https://insideclimatenews.org/news/24062022/concrete-is-worse-for-the-climate-than-flying-why-arent-more-people-talking-about-it/). With the use of traditional building materials like concrete increasing embodied emission levels, construction businesses are now turning to new, sustainable alternatives to reduce the carbon footprint of their building projects. Here are 16 building materials that can help make construction sustainable and protect the environment (while also saving construction costs). 1. Bamboo --------- Bamboo has already been used in construction for hundreds of years. An extremely flexible material, it’s very easy for construction firms to source, as this rapidly-growing plant – the fastest-growing plant in the world – can be found in multiple continents. What makes bamboo so sustainable is how little of it needs to be wasted. An entire bamboo stem can be used in construction, and as it’s naturally biodegradable, any construction waste can be disposed. 2. Mass timber -------------- Short for ‘massive timber’ and also referred to as ‘structural timber’, this material is the result of glueing, doweling or nailing pieces of softer wood together in order to form larger, stronger pieces. The process results in a material with the strength ratings of wood or concrete that is significantly lighter in weight. Mass timber has generated plenty excitement for its potential implications in sustainable construction. In 2019, [Mjøstårnet](https://www.vox.com/energy-and-environment/2020/1/15/21058051/climate-change-building-materials-mass-timber-cross-laminated-clt), a tower in Norway, was certified as the world’s tallest timber building at the time, at 18 storeys 85.4 metres in height. It has since been surpassed by [Ascent](https://www.dezeen.com/2023/03/29/worlds-tallest-buildings-mass-timber-revolution/), a 25-storey, 86.6-metre-tall tower in the US. Both of these buildings were created with mass timber. 3. Cob ------ Cob is made by crushing together subsoil, sand, straw, and sometimes lime. This natural building material has almost zero embodied carbon, and is both strong and durable, with the oldest known cob house in the UK [dating back to the 15 th century](https://wessexsurveyors.co.uk/2023/03/cob-houses/). This sustainable material [has been adopted and re-engineered by researchers](https://www.dezeen.com/2019/03/11/cobbauge-cob-building-sustainable-architecture/) to a new, modern mixture with the ability to absorb and trap heat, known as CobBauge. The goal is for this new mixture to become an effective alternative to concrete and a viable option for low carbon construction. 4. Recycled steel ----------------- Along with fresh, natural materials with low-embodied carbon, construction firms are also looking to recycle existing materials, with steel a prime candidate. Construction is already one of the biggest consumers of steel and it’s easy to recycle, with around [40% of all steel production involving the use of recycled material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/B9780123964595000064#:~:text=Currently%20about%2040%25%20of%20the,the%20steel%20from%20recycled%20scrap.). Recycled steel can be used to build structural components from beams, bars and columns. Steel buildings can be recycled almost entirely, with very little waste, and the resulting recycled steel can have the same strength and durability. 5. Recycled plastic ------------------- Plastic – one of the most widely used, consumed and disposed substances on the planet – has a variety of applications for construction. Its water, corrosion-resistance and thermal insulation qualities make it an ideal material for the construction of houses, and while it doesn’t have the strength of steel or even wood, it can be mixed with other materials to compensate for this. Recycled plastic is used to build components like lumber, fencing, floor and roof tiles, bricks, concrete, and even gravel. [![Image 3](https://www.ube.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/On-page-banners-static.jpg)](http://www.ube.ac.uk/whats-happening/articles/category/sustainability/) 6. Rammed earth --------------- The process of creating rammed earth has been used for thousands of years. It involves ramming together a mixture of aggregates (such as gravel, clay and sand) into a formwork. This formwork can then be moulded to create walls, which, once dried, become solid. These walls are extremely durable, require little maintenance and can last for hundreds of years. This ancient technique is naturally sustainable and energy efficient, as it involves using locally sourced materials and can be easily reused and recycled. It also has high thermal mass, meaning it’s effective at absorbing and storing heat, which can help reduce energy usage and expenses. 7. Cork ------- Buildings and infrastructure might not be the first thing that comes to mind when you think of cork, but that could be set to change. This natural material is being touted as an option for sustainable construction, and has been used to create insulation, tiles, shingles, wall coverings, roofs and countertops. Rather than cutting down a tree, cork can be harvested from stripping bark and, thanks to its cell structure, it actually stores carbon. As a sustainable building material, its low weight and soft structure make it easier for builders to work with and manipulate. 8. Recycled glass ----------------- Glass is yet another substance than can be reused and recycled in construction. Recycling it is involves far less energy consumption than producing new glass. It also reduces the need for virgin materials, and loses none of its strength of quality in the recycling process. 9. Straw bale ------------- Bales of straw are another sustainable material with potential energy saving benefits. It’s already being used for insulation – it has three times the R-value (a measurement for heat resistance) of traditional insulation – and can be tied together with bamboo or wood to create walls. From a sustainability perspective, bales of straw are 100% biodegradable, and have very low embodied energy. Straw has already been used in the construction of houses, theatres and university buildings, and these structures could stand for over a century if they’re properly maintained. 10. Earth blocks ---------------- Often referred to as compressed earth blocks (CEB), this sustainable material is similar to cob in its composition. It’s a mixture of dry subsoil, aggregate, sand and clay. These natural resources are compressed together through high pressure and dampening to form a block shape, which can function as a brick. Earth blocks have a naturally low carbon footprint owing to their composition, making them a viable and sustainable option for building houses. They’re also fire resistant, and as they’re composed of water, they can easily be produced at a construction site. However, one potential weakness of earth blocks is their low tensile strength – the maximum amount of stress they’re able to bear. They’re also known to lose strength and stability when in contact with water for long periods of time. 11. Sheep’s wool ---------------- Unlike some of the other sustainable materials listed, sheep’s wool has a very specific application as an effective insulator. Sheep’s wool has evolved in order to keep sheep warm in extremely cold weather conditions, and construction confirms can [benefit from its ability to absorb and release moisture](https://koruarchitects.co.uk/natural-insulation-benefits-sheeps-wool), regulate humidity, stabilise temperatures and ultimately purify the air. Amid concerns around rising energy costs and demands for improved air quality, sheep’s wool is an insulation alternative is something construction firms should take note of. 12. Clay brick -------------- Clay is another green material that can be easily sourced and has been used in building projects for hundreds of years. For the modern construction industry, the most promising application of this versatile material is in the creation of clay bricks that are hard-wearing, recyclable, low maintenance and have strong thermal insulation qualities. What’s more, along with having low embodied energy, clay bricks also align with the lean methodology, in that they enable the reduction of waste and can be easily sourced locally to a building site. 13. Recycled rubber ------------------- While used tyres most often end up in landfills, rubber has a variety of applications for construction, from landscaping, asphalt and flooring to potentially [a sustainable replacement](https://specifymagazine.co.uk/2024/01/06/recycled-tyre-rubber-to-be-transformed-into-sustainable-concrete/) for traditional concrete. This new concrete-like material, dubbed ‘rubbercrete’, is made by replacing the fine aggregate in traditional concrete with crumb rubber from used tires. **Learn more:**[12 sustainable alternatives to traditional concrete](https://www.ube.ac.uk/whats-happening/articles/concrete-alternatives/) 14. Newspaperwood ----------------- Despite the fact that newspaper circulation is declining, in the UK they’re still read by [1 in 4 adults every 15 days](https://medialandscapes.org/country/united-kingdom/media/print). The majority of newspapers are recyclable and compostable, and they can even be used by construction firms to create a substitute for wood. Newspaperwood, as the name suggests, is a wood-like material made from coating sheets of newspaper together with glue into logs. It can even be varnished so it resembles wood, although many prefer newspaperwood’s distinct appearance. Just like traditional wood, it’s versatile, durable and can be made waterproof. 15. Plant-based polyurethane rigid foam --------------------------------------- One of the most exciting eco-friendly building materials on this list, polyurethane is a rigid foam made out of foamed polymers that, while prevalent in the construction industry, are problematic in terms of sustainability. This indispensable material, crucial to effective insulation of buildings, is largely made from petrochemicals and difficult to dispose of. Plant-based foams or ‘biofoams’ have emerged as a new alternative with a lower carbon footprint than traditional foam. They’re made from a variety of natural fibres, including bamboo and hemp plants. 16. Ferrock ----------- Ferrock is a green building material often used as a substitute for cement. Created from a mixture of waste steel dust silica, it’s both stronger than concrete and extremely sustainable, being that the process of producing it causes no damage to the environment. What has people most excited about ferrock is that it is CO2 negative – in other words, instead of emitting CO2 when drying, it actually [absorbs and binds to it](https://mperryassociates.com/2022/11/16/ferrock-for-building/). _Sustainability isn’t a passing trend – it’s here to stay and is constantly evolving. If you want to inspire and action change in your career, UCEM’s MSc Innovation in Sustainable Built Environments will give you the skills you need, both now and in the future._ _Find out more:_[MSc Innovation in Sustainable Built Environments – University College of Estate Management](https://www.ube.ac.uk/postgraduate-programmes/msc-innovation-in-sustainable-built-environments/) [![Image 4](https://www.ube.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/On-page-banners-static.jpg)](http://www.ube.ac.uk/whats-happening/articles/category/sustainability/) Articles -------- [Show all +](https://www.ube.ac.uk/whats-happening/articles/) [![Image 5](https://www.ube.ac.uk/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/Online-vs-in-person-study-which-one-is-right-for-you-thumbnail-400x211.jpg) ### Online vs in-person study: which one is right for you? Please let us know if you need a deep-dive on any of the following: ![loader](https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/accets/frontend/img/ajax_loader.gif) ## KEY MARKET INSIGHTS ![Play Audio](https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/accets/frontend/img/Play_gif.gif) The U.S. green building materials market size was valued at USD 88.2 billion in 2022 and is projected to record a CAGR of 12.7% during the forecast period. Green building materials are eco-friendly construction products designed to have minimal environmental impact throughout their lifecycle. These materials are often sourced sustainably, manufactured using energy-efficient processes, and are recyclable or biodegradable. Some examples include recycled steel, bamboo, reclaimed wood, and energy-efficient insulation materials. Green buildings aim to create an energy-efficient, environmentally responsible structure and promote a healthier living space. The increasing demand for green buildings in the U.S. is expected to drive market growth during the forecast period. Green building can be described as creating buildings and pursuing resource and environmentally-conscious construction, operation, design, dismantling, conversion, and maintenance processes. The demand for eco-friendly building materials is increasing as more sustainable materials are used in building green buildings. Moreover, the rising demand for these materials is becoming popular in many construction projects as they reduce the environmental impact of building materials in terms of installation, manufacturing, transportation, processing, disposal, and recycling. The increasing preference for [green building materials](../green-building-materials-market-102932) over traditional building materials is expected to bolster market growth. The construction industry was severely affected by COVID-19, which hindered the U.S. market. Moreover, this industry experienced its worst phase in recent decades due to the pandemic. It also experienced consequences such as a shortage of building materials, logistical challenges, and labour shortages in the U.S. ## **U.S. Green Building Materials Market Trends** ***Rising Awareness about Environmentally Friendly Practices to Drive Market Growth*** Green building materials are known for their energy-efficient properties, such as better [insulation](../insulation-market-102423) and reflective roofing. These features reduce energy consumption in buildings, addressing the growing emphasis on energy efficiency and conservation. Moreover, the U.S. government has implemented various initiatives and regulations promoting sustainable construction activities. Incentives, tax breaks, and stricter building codes for energy efficiency drive the adoption of this type of building materials. In addition, there is growing demand from environmentally conscious consumers who prioritize sustainable and eco-friendly living. This demand influences builders and developers to use green materials, meeting consumers' expectations that value sustainability. Such factors are expected to drive the U.S. market growth during the forecast period. ## **U.S. Green Building Materials Market Growth Factors** ***Increasing Renovation and Construction of New Infrastructure** **to Aid Market Growth*** There is growing awareness and emphasis on sustainability and environmental conservation. As a result, both consumers and businesses are increasingly opting for construction materials that have a reduced environmental impact. Green building materials, such as recycled steel, reclaimed wood, and energy-efficient insulation, align with the principle of sustainable construction. Furthermore, the demand for energy-efficient and environmentally friendly structures has increased. The product often contributes to improved energy efficiency and indoor air quality, aligning with a broader societal shift toward environmentally conscious practices. Moreover, regarding environmental considerations, economic factors are driving the growth of this market. Long-term cost savings associated with energy-efficient materials and technologies make them attractive for both residential and commercial construction projects. As more property owners recognize the value of sustainable building in terms of reduced operational costs and increased property value, it will drive the demand for the product. ## **RESTRAINING FACTORS** ***High Capital Investment Related to the Product May Restrain Market Growth*** High capital investment associated with the product is one of the major challenges faced by the market. Green building materials are alternatives to conventional building materials and, therefore, need to be of really good quality. Thus, these materials are quite expensive. In addition, very few companies in the market deal with green buildings and green building policies, as they need expertise on this topic. This significantly hinders the growth of the U.S. green building materials market. ## **U.S. Green Building Materials Market Segmentation Analysis** ### **By Application Analysis** By application, the market is divided into insulation, roofing, flooring, and others.  The roofing segment held the largest U.S. green building materials market share in 2022 and dominated the market during the forecast period. Due to the increasing adoption of green [roofing materials](../roofing-materials-market-102859). Green roofs are gaining traction as nature-based solutions (NBS) to address various environmental and socio-economic issues related to climate change. The flooring segment is likely to grow significantly during the forecast period. The U.S. Green Building Council’s Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED) certification system has significantly influenced the adoption of green building practices. Flooring materials that contribute to LEED points, such as those with recycled content or low VOC (volatile organic compound) emission, are favored in construction projects aiming for LEED certification. The insulation segment is expected to witness moderate growth during the forecast period as it is an energy-saving measure that provides resistance to heat flow. Naturally, heat flows from a warmer room to a cooler one through the many surfaces in a building, such as ducts, roofs, walls, etc. ### **By End-use Industry Analysis** In terms of end-use industry, the market is bifurcated into non-residential and residential. The residential segment is poised to dominate the market during the forecast period owing to the rising number of energy-efficient construction policies and building laws. Growing awareness about the environmental benefits of sustainable building materials and increasing adoption of sustainable building practices in the residential sector are the major factors further driving the growth of this segment. The non-residential segment is expected to grow significantly during the forecast period due to the increasing need for sustainable and eco-friendly solutions. ## **List of Key Companies in U.S. Green Building Materials Market** With regard to the competitive landscape, the U.S. market showcases the presence of established and emerging producing companies. The major players include PPG Industries, Inc., BASF SE, Sika AG, Forbo International SA, and E. I. du Pont de Nemours and Company, among others. Industry players are competing in the price and application areas of the product. They are emphasizing product innovation, acquisitions, collaborations, and social marketing. ### **LIST OF KEY COMPANIES PROFILED:** ### **KEY INDUSTRY DEVELOPMENTS:** ## **REPORT COVERAGE** An Infographic Representation of U.S. Green Building Materials Market [![U.S. Green Building Materials Market](https://fbi-reports-file.s3.amazonaws.com/fbi_l/info_images/109063.png "U.S. Green Building Materials Market") ![Full View](https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/accets/frontend/img/full-view.svg) View Full Infographic](#) To get information on various segments, [share your queries with us](https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/enquiry/queries/u-s-green-building-materials-market-109063?utm_medium=info) An Infographic Representation of U.S. Green Building Materials Market ![U.S. Green Building Materials Market](https://fbi-reports-file.s3.amazonaws.com/fbi_l/info_images/109063.png "U.S. Green Building Materials Market") ![Full View](https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/accets/frontend/img/full-view.svg) To get information on various segments, [share your queries with us](https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/enquiry/queries/u-s-green-building-materials-market-109063?utm_medium=info) The study report thoroughly examines key factors, such as leading companies, applications, and products. enriched_points.expanded_content: The construction industry faces increasing pressure to adopt sustainable building materials such as bamboo, recycled steel, and reclaimed wood, reflecting a shift towards greener practices.
Climate change necessitates urgent action from the construction sector to lower carbon emissions, prompting the exploration of low-carbon alternatives to traditional cement.

enriched_points.point_title: Regulatory pressures on the construction industry
enriched_points.expanded_content: Governments are implementing stricter regulations and incentives promoting sustainable construction, pushing builders to integrate eco-friendly materials and practices in order to comply with new standards.
